title: Sub-shot-noise emission statistics of a CW-excited single photon source
authors: G. Gavello, G. Petrini, I. Ruo Berchera, M. Gramegna, E. Moreva, P. Traina, M. Ziino, M. Genovese, P. Olivero, J. Forneris, I. P. Degiovanni
abstract: Shot noise sets a fundamental limit on the sensitivity of classical optical measurements, with coherent emitters achieving the lowest possible shot-noise level. Emission from sub-Poissonian light provides a pathway to surpass this limit, and single-photon sources provide a natural platform for generating such light. However, it is commonly assumed that continuously excited single-photon sources exhibit Poissonian statistics. In this work, a theoretical model of a continuously driven two-level single-photon source is developed, treating both excitation and radiative decay as stochastic processes. The analysis demonstrates that photon emission can display sub-Poissonian statistics when excitation and decay rates are comparable, showing that continuous excitation does not inherently preclude nonclassical emission. The model is further extended to include finite detection efficiency and detector dead time, illustrating how these practical non-idealities can affect the experimental observation of sub-Poissonian statistics.
summary: This paper proves that single-photon sources under continuous excitation can produce sub-Poissonian light when excitation and decay rates are balanced. It also provides a quantitative guide for experimentalists to distinguish true nonclassical emission from artifacts caused by detector dead time and low collection efficiency.
main_problem: The paper challenges the assumption that continuous-wave (CW) excited single-photon sources (SPSs) inherently exhibit Poissonian statistics and investigates how detection non-idealities affect the observation of sub-Poissonian light.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that sub-Poissonian statistics can occur when excitation and decay rates are comparable, reaching a minimum Fano factor of 0.5, and show that while losses reduce the visibility of this signature, detector dead time can create artificial sub-Poissonian artifacts.
method: A theoretical framework using Probability Generating Functions (PGF) and Laplace transforms to derive the moments of photon statistics from stochastic absorption and emission processes.
model_or_system: A theoretical model of a two-level single-photon source subject to continuous optical excitation, where both photon absorption and radiative decay are treated as independent stochastic Poissonian processes.
key_observables: Fano factor (ratio of variance to mean), mean photon number, and photon number variance.
important_parameters: Excitation rate (mu1), decay rate (mu2), detection efficiency (eta), and detector dead time (D).
main_assumptions: Each absorption-emission cycle is assumed to be independent, and the analysis focuses on the long-time limit (T to infinity).
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Fano factor dip below 1.0 at the rate ratio of 1; Figure 2 shows how decreasing detection efficiency reduces the depth of the Fano factor minimum; Figure 3 shows how detector dead time introduces asymmetry and can mask the intrinsic sub-Poissonian minimum.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of CW-excited statistics, develops a stochastic model using PGFs and Laplace transforms, extends the model to include realistic detection losses and dead time, and concludes with an analysis of experimental implications.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems as it provides a rigorous way to distinguish intrinsic quantum correlations from experimental artifacts in photon-counting statistics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Plot of the quantity ξ∞, defined as the ratio of the variance to the mean number of emitted photons by a continuously excited single-photon source in the large-time limit, as a function of the ratio µ1/µ2.

FIG. 2. Plot of the quantity ξ∞(η), defined as the ratio of the variance to the mean number of emitted photons by a continuously excited single-photon source in the large-time limit in the presence of optical losses. Losses are quantified by the detection efficiency η. The ratio is displayed as a function of µ1/µ2, with each colored curve corresponding to a different η value indicated in the legend.

FIG. 3. Plot of the quantity ξ∞(D,η), defined as the ratio of the variance to the mean number of emitted photons by a continuously excited single-photon source in the large-time limit in the pres- ence of optical losses and finite detector dead time D. The ratio is displayed as a function of µ1/µ2, with each colored curve corre- sponding to a different D value indicated in the legend, expressed in unit of the characteristic lifetime τ of the single-photon source. Since the impact of optical losses has already been considered, all curves are calculated for a fixed optical efficiency: η = 0.5.
